name: Bengaluru

trends.title: Dry spell and construction dust
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 48 µg/m³ — 3.2x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Humidity was declining (54% → 50%), reducing natural moisture suppression of dust and allowing particles to stay airborne longer.
PM10 was elevated at 89 µg/m³ (above the 76 baseline), suggesting road dust or construction activity contributed.
news: Metro Pink Line nearing trial phase — active construction on elevated stretch (Construction World)
No rain recorded since early March — dry conditions favored dust buildup

trends.title: Hailstorm stirred up particles before clearing
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 46 µg/m³ — 3.1x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: High PM2.5/PM10 ratio — fine particles dominate, pointing to combustion sources (vehicles, burning) rather than dust.
Temperature dropped to 21.8°C (vs 25.6°C avg) — suggests a storm or cold front.
news: Mar 17: Hailstorm hit Sanjayanagar, Yelahanka, Devanahalli (Deccan Herald)
IMD issued thunderstorm warnings for Mar 17-21 across Karnataka (Zee News)

trends.title: Post-hailstorm air cleared up
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 28 µg/m³ — still 1.9x the WHO limit
hypotheses: Humidity was 62% (above 55% average) — likely rainfall washed particulates from the air.
Ozone elevated at 48 µg/m³ — sunny conditions driving photochemical smog.
news: Continued rain after Mar 17 hailstorm washed out residual particles (IMD warnings through Mar 21)
Ugadi (Kannada New Year) on Mar 19 — reduced weekday activity

trends.title: Pollution dropped back after one-day anomaly
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 36 µg/m³ — still 2.4x the WHO limit
hypotheses: The previous day (60 µg/m³) was a sharp one-day spike — well above the 36 average. Without a sustained source, concentrations naturally dispersed overnight.
news: Mar 23 recorded the month's highest PM2.5 (60 µg/m³) — fell to 36 by Mar 24

trends.title: Pre-monsoon showers kept air clean
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 28 µg/m³ — still 1.8x the WHO limit
hypotheses: A sustained 4-day period below average suggests a favorable weather pattern — likely improved ventilation from wind or intermittent showers dispersing pollutants.
news: Pre-monsoon convective season established — 90-100% thunderstorm probability (Oneindia)
Light to moderate rain across Bengaluru Urban and surrounding districts (IMD)

narrative: Bengaluru's air is 2.4x the safe limit — moderate by Indian standards but never actually safe. Metro construction dust is a persistent driver. The month saw a hailstorm that briefly cleaned the air, a mysterious one-day spike to 60 µg/m³, an IPL match that spiked traffic, and the early signs of pre-monsoon relief. Even the cleanest day (17 µg/m³) was still above WHO limits.

stories.event: Metro construction dust pushed mid-March to near month's worst
stories.impact: PM2.5 hit 48 µg/m³ on Mar 13 — 3.2x the safe limit. Dry conditions (humidity dropping from 65% to 50%) let dust linger.
stories.detail: Namma Metro Phase 2 construction is happening in crowded residential areas without proper dust management. Citizen Matters reported dust suppression measures are not being followed properly.

stories.event: Hailstorm hit the city, then air cleared for 3 days
stories.impact: PM2.5 spiked to 46 during the storm, then dropped to 28 as rain washed out particles over the following days.
stories.detail: Hail was reported in Sanjayanagar, Yelahanka, and Devanahalli. IMD issued thunderstorm warnings for Mar 17-21. This was the first spell of pre-monsoon showers. Ugadi was celebrated on Mar 19.

stories.event: Worst day of the month — PM2.5 hit 60, then crashed back
stories.impact: PM2.5 spiked to 60 µg/m³ (4x the safe limit) then dropped to 36 overnight — a 41% fall.
stories.detail: No specific event found for this one-day anomaly. The spike was city-wide across 39 stations, suggesting a regional dust event rather than a local source.

stories.event: Pre-monsoon showers kept air below average for 4 days
stories.impact: PM2.5 averaged 31 µg/m³ — still 2x the safe limit, but the cleanest sustained stretch of the month.
stories.detail: IMD confirmed the pre-monsoon convective season had established over Karnataka, with 90-100% thunderstorm probability.

stories.event: IPL match day — traffic spiked, so did pollution
stories.impact: PM2.5 jumped from 32 to 43 µg/m³. The only spike in an otherwise clean first week of April.
stories.detail: RCB vs CSK at Chinnaswamy Stadium. Police banned parking on MG Road and Queens Road, issued formal traffic advisory. Massive crowds converged on the city center.

stories.event: 35.2°C heatwave — but PM2.5 actually dropped
stories.impact: Bengaluru recorded its lowest PM2.5 of the month (17 µg/m³ — near WHO safe levels) on one of its hottest days.
stories.detail: The 35.2°C reading was 1.5°C above normal. Hot air rising creates strong vertical mixing that can disperse pollutants — heat doesn't always mean more pollution.

name: Mumbai

trends.title: Construction and traffic spiked early March pollution
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 48 µg/m³ — 3.2x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: NO2 elevated at 37 µg/m³ (vs 25 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
news: BMC acted against 1000+ construction sites for dust-control violations (Deccan Herald)
Hotspots: Kanjurmarg AQI 309, Ghatkopar 183, Vikhroli 163 (Deccan Herald)

trends.title: BMC enforcement and sea breeze cleaned the air
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 13 µg/m³ — near WHO safe levels
hypotheses: Very low PM2.5/PM10 ratio — coarse particles dominate, pointing to construction or road dust rather than combustion.
news: PM2.5 down 14%, PM10 down 17% year-on-year due to BMC enforcement (Deccan Herald)

trends.title: Mid-week traffic and construction spike
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 48 µg/m³ — 3.2x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: NO2 elevated at 31 µg/m³ (vs 25 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
news: Clear skies and heat on Mar 27 — AQI 89 'moderate' with dust hotspots (Free Press Journal)

trends.title: Rainfall brought ultra-clean air
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 10 µg/m³ — near WHO safe levels
hypotheses: A sustained 12-day period below average suggests a favorable weather pattern — likely improved ventilation from wind or intermittent showers dispersing pollutants.
news: AQI dropped to 21 (Good) on Mar 31 — ultra-clean air amid rainfall predictions (Free Press Journal)
Light rain on Mar 31 brought relief from heat and washed out pollutants (Free Press Journal)

trends.title: Pollution returned as dry heat set in
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 43 µg/m³ — 2.9x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Dry conditions (48% vs 66% average) — no rain washout, more dust resuspension.
news: Mumbai AQI 100 (Moderate) on Apr 10; Navi Mumbai hit 201 (Severe) on Apr 9 (AQI.in)
Mumbai ranked among most polluted cities globally on Apr 9 (IQAir)

narrative: Mumbai had two clear phases this month. Early March was dirty (PM2.5 up to 48, driven by construction and traffic). Then rain arrived on Mar 28 and the air became extraordinarily clean — AQI hit 21 on Mar 31, the lowest in months. But by April 9, dry heat brought it all back.

stories.event: BMC crackdown on 1,073 construction sites
stories.impact: PM2.5 averaged 44 µg/m³ despite enforcement. Hotspots: Kanjurmarg AQI 309, Ghatkopar 183.
stories.detail: BMC issued stop-work notices to 1,073 construction sites violating dust-control norms. PM2.5 was down 14% year-on-year, but still 3x the safe limit.

stories.event: Pollution crept back up over 10 days — peaking at 48
stories.impact: PM2.5 rose gradually from 13 to 48 µg/m³ over 10 days as dry weather returned.
stories.detail: After BMC enforcement brought brief relief, pollution steadily rebuilt. Mumbai had only 7 'good' air days in 5 years — chronic pollution is the norm, not the exception. Construction dust is the primary driver.

stories.event: Rainfall brought ultra-clean air — AQI hit 21
stories.impact: PM2.5 dropped to 10 µg/m³ — below the WHO safe limit for the first time. AQI 21 on Mar 31.
stories.detail: Light rain on Mar 31 washed out pollutants. Mumbai's air was cleaner than most European cities for nearly two weeks.

stories.event: Dry heat brought pollution right back
stories.impact: PM2.5 surged to 43 µg/m³ by Apr 10 — the highest since late March. Navi Mumbai AQI hit 201 (Severe).
stories.detail: Mumbai ranked among the most polluted cities globally on Apr 9. The clean spell ended as rain stopped and dry heat returned.

name: Delhi

trends.title: Spring dust and traffic pushed pollution up
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 142 µg/m³ — 9.4x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: NO2 elevated at 66 µg/m³ (vs 49 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
news: Spring season — westerly dust from Rajasthan carries coarse particles into Delhi (IQAir)
Vehicle emissions account for ~30% of Delhi's PM2.5 year-round (Down to Earth)

trends.title: Rain and wind brought temporary relief
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 42 µg/m³ — still 2.8x the WHO limit
hypotheses: PM2.5 dropped from 45 to 42 µg/m³ — a brief dip likely driven by temporary weather improvement or reduced local emissions.
news: Light showers and thunderstorms on Mar 15 — winds 30-50 kmph (IMD via Asianet Newsable)
Western disturbance brought unseasonal rain to Delhi-NCR (8pm News)

trends.title: Best AQI in 161 days after overnight rain
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 29 µg/m³ — still 2.0x the WHO limit
hypotheses: High PM2.5/PM10 ratio — fine particles dominate, pointing to combustion sources (vehicles, burning) rather than dust.
Humidity was 79% (above 64% average) — likely rainfall washed particulates from the air.
news: AQI dropped to 93 (Satisfactory) — best reading in 161 days (NewsX)
Coldest March day in 6 years after overnight rain (Outlook Traveller)
Western disturbance brought exceptional rain band across north India (Legacy IAS)

trends.title: Dust storm pushed AQI into hazardous range
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 126 µg/m³ — 8.4x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: NO2 elevated at 66 µg/m³ (vs 49 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
news: Spring dust storms from Rajasthan and western India (IQAir, StudyHub)

trends.title: Massive dust storm — AQI crossed 500
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 124 µg/m³ — 8.3x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Dry conditions (55% vs 64% average) — no rain washout, more dust resuspension.
NO2 elevated at 63 µg/m³ (vs 49 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
news: AQI exceeded 500 on Apr 2 — PM10 above 800 µg/m³, hazardous conditions (IQAir)
Delhi ranked among top 10 most polluted cities globally on Apr 3 (IQAir)
Overwhelmingly driven by coarse dust, not combustion (IQAir)

trends.title: Bounce after an unusually clean day
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 27 µg/m³ — still 1.8x the WHO limit
hypotheses: NO2 low at 36 µg/m³ (vs 49 baseline) — reduced traffic, possibly holiday or road closure.
Ozone elevated at 37 µg/m³ — sunny conditions driving photochemical smog.
news: Apr 6 recorded just 27 µg/m³ — one of Delhi's cleanest days this month
Western disturbance approaching — humidity surged to 85% on Apr 7 (IMD)

trends.title: Dust spiked then winds cleared the air overnight
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 25 µg/m³ — still 1.7x the WHO limit
hypotheses: Very low PM2.5/PM10 ratio — coarse particles dominate, pointing to construction or road dust rather than combustion.
NO2 low at 35 µg/m³ (vs 49 baseline) — reduced traffic, possibly holiday or road closure.
Ozone elevated at 37 µg/m³ — sunny conditions driving photochemical smog.
news: AQI hit 383 on Apr 9 morning — PM10 dominated (AQI.in)
Delhi AQI described as 'dangerously volatile' in spring 2026 (The Patriot)
Winds 22-26 kmph on Apr 10 dispersed accumulated dust (Air Quality EWS)

narrative: Delhi's spring air is a rollercoaster — dust storms from Rajasthan push PM2.5 to 9x the safe limit one day, rain clears it the next. The city swung between 25 and 142 µg/m³ this month. PM2.5 exceeded the WHO limit on every single day, even the 'good' ones.

stories.event: Spring dust and traffic opened the month at 9x safe limit
stories.impact: PM2.5 hit 142 µg/m³ on the first day — the worst reading of the entire month.
stories.detail: Westerly dust from Rajasthan combined with vehicle emissions. Delhi starts spring in a pollution trap that only rain can break.

stories.event: Holi doubled pollution overnight
stories.impact: PM2.5 jumped from 58 to 119 µg/m³ — doubling in a single day, 8x the safe limit.
stories.detail: Holi (festival of colours) was celebrated on Mar 14 with Holika Dahan bonfires the night before. Firecrackers and bonfires are known pollution drivers during festivals. Rain on Mar 15 brought it back to 45.

stories.event: Rain brought the best air quality in 161 days
stories.impact: PM2.5 dropped to 29 µg/m³. AQI hit 93 — first 'Satisfactory' rating since October 2025.
stories.detail: A western disturbance — unusual for its timing and 1,000 km rain band — brought overnight showers. Also the coldest March day in 6 years.

stories.event: Dust returned — 3 days above 100 µg/m³
stories.impact: PM2.5 hit 126 on Mar 23, stayed above 100 for 3 straight days. 8x the safe limit.
stories.detail: After the Mar 19 rain relief, pollution rebuilt over 4 days as dry conditions returned and traffic/construction resumed. The relief lasted exactly 4 days.
sources: OAQ station data — no external event identified

stories.event: Massive dust storm — AQI crossed 500
stories.impact: PM10 exceeded 800 µg/m³. Delhi ranked among the world's 10 most polluted cities.
stories.detail: The spike was overwhelmingly coarse dust — not combustion — carried by westerly winds from Rajasthan. Hazardous conditions for two days.

stories.event: Coldest April day in 11 years cleared the air
stories.impact: Temperature hit 28.2°C — 6.9°C below normal. AQI dropped to 93 (Satisfactory).
stories.detail: A western disturbance brought rain and wind. But by Apr 9, dust was back at 105 µg/m³. Relief lasted less than 48 hours.

stories.event: 80 µg/m³ swing in 24 hours — dust then wind
stories.impact: PM2.5 crashed from 105 to 25 overnight. From 'Severe' to near safe in one day.
stories.detail: Winds of 22-26 kmph dispersed accumulated dust. Delhi's spring AQI has been described as 'dangerously volatile.'

name: Hyderabad

trends.title: Dry heat built up pollution
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 40 µg/m³ — 2.6x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Humidity dipped as low as 44% during this period (well below the 58% average), creating dry conditions that favor dust buildup.
news: Temperatures 32-37°C across Telangana with no rain (IMD via PingTV)

trends.title: Unseasonal rain cleared the air
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 22 µg/m³ — near WHO safe levels
hypotheses: Humidity was 63% (above 58% average) — likely rainfall washed particulates from the air.
news: Unseasonal rains with lightning and gusty winds on Mar 17 (PingTV)
IMD warned of winds 41-61 kmph — residents advised to stay indoors
Ugadi celebrated Mar 19 across Telangana (Hyderabad Mail)

trends.title: Construction dust spiked pollution
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 44 µg/m³ — 2.9x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: PM10 was elevated at 87 µg/m³ (above the 78 baseline), suggesting road dust or construction activity contributed.
news: AQI reached 146 (Poor) on March 27 (AQI.in)
Residents near Golconda Chowrasta complained of severe construction dust (Hyderabad Mail)

narrative: Hyderabad's air is the steadiest of the 6 cities — 2.1x the WHO limit with less day-to-day volatility than Delhi or Jaipur. Unseasonal rain around Ugadi gave the longest clean stretch of the month, but construction dust quickly brought pollution back.

stories.event: Dry heat pushed pollution to month's peak
stories.impact: PM2.5 averaged 37 µg/m³ — the highest sustained stretch of the month.
stories.detail: Temperatures 32-37°C with no rain let dust and emissions accumulate across the city.

stories.event: Unseasonal rain + Ugadi gave a week of cleaner air
stories.impact: PM2.5 dropped to 22 µg/m³ on Mar 16 and stayed low for 7 days — the longest clean stretch of the month.
stories.detail: Unseasonal rains with lightning and gusty winds (41-61 kmph) swept through Telangana. Ugadi was celebrated on Mar 19 with reduced weekday activity.

stories.event: Construction dust spiked pollution — residents complained
stories.impact: PM2.5 jumped to 44 µg/m³ — 2.9x the safe limit. AQI hit 146 on Mar 27.
stories.detail: Residents near Golconda Chowrasta reported severe dust from construction. Elderly, children, and patients faced respiratory problems.

stories.event: Late-March showers brought April into a calmer pattern
stories.impact: PM2.5 averaged 27 µg/m³ for a week — no dramatic spikes.
stories.detail: Late evening thundershowers around end of March reset conditions. April was steadier than March.

name: Chennai

trends.title: Dry season dust from industrial north
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 28 µg/m³
hypotheses: Very low PM2.5/PM10 ratio — coarse particles dominate, pointing to construction or road dust rather than combustion.
news: Manali-Ennore petrochemical and refinery corridor — persistent SO2 and PM10 source (AAQR)
Dry Jan-Mar season sees worst air quality citywide (AAQR)

trends.title: Road dust and construction spiked coarse particles
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 27 µg/m³
hypotheses: PM10 was elevated at 66 µg/m³ (above the 50 baseline), suggesting road dust or construction activity contributed.
news: Manali recorded AQI 154 (Unhealthy) in late March (AQI.in)
Metro rail construction generates significant dust and air quality impacts (Nature Scientific Reports)

trends.title: Sea breeze and lower activity brought relief
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 10 µg/m³ — near WHO safe levels
hypotheses: A sustained 5-day period below average suggests a favorable weather pattern — likely improved ventilation from wind or intermittent showers dispersing pollutants.
news: Summer sea breeze pattern helps disperse coastal city pollutants (AAQR)

trends.title: Summer heat spiked pollution
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 32 µg/m³ — 2.2x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Humidity was declining (85% → 73%), reducing natural moisture suppression of dust and allowing particles to stay airborne longer.
PM10 was elevated at 60 µg/m³ (above the 50 baseline), suggesting road dust or construction activity contributed.
news: Chennai AQI reached 135 (Poor) on Apr 9, 120 on Apr 10 — PM2.5 dominant (AQI.in)

narrative: Chennai is the closest to safe air among the 6 cities — just 1.1x the WHO limit on average. The Manali-Ennore industrial corridor in the north drives most pollution. The story this month: generally clean conditions with a late spike as summer heat arrived.

stories.event: Dry season pushed pollution to month's highest
stories.impact: PM2.5 averaged 25 µg/m³ — modest by Indian standards but still above the WHO limit.
stories.detail: The Manali-Ennore petrochemical and refinery corridor is a persistent source of SO2 and PM10. Dry Jan-Mar is the worst season for Chennai's air.

stories.event: Mid-March: air dropped to 4 µg/m³ — some of India's cleanest
stories.impact: PM2.5 hit 4 µg/m³. For context, that's cleaner than most Scandinavian cities.
stories.detail: Sea breeze and reduced activity drove Chennai well below the WHO limit. The coastal geography helps — onshore winds push pollutants inland.

stories.event: Late March spike — Manali industrial corridor lit up
stories.impact: PM2.5 rose from 15 to 27 µg/m³ — still modest, but the highest stretch of the month.
stories.detail: The Manali-Ennore petrochemical corridor and metro rail construction are persistent dust and SO2 sources. Manali station recorded AQI 154 (Unhealthy) in late March.

stories.event: Summer heat spiked pollution to 32
stories.impact: PM2.5 jumped to 32 µg/m³ — AQI hit 135 (Poor). Highest of the month.
stories.detail: As humidity dropped from 85% to 73%, particles stayed airborne longer. Chennai AQI reached 120-135, classified as 'Poor.'

name: Jaipur

trends.title: Desert dust and traffic in dry heat
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 172 µg/m³ — 11.4x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Dry conditions (37% vs 50% average) — no rain washout, more dust resuspension.
NO2 elevated at 113 µg/m³ (vs 65 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
SO2 elevated at 9.0 µg/m³ (vs 6.3 baseline) — industrial emissions or power generation.
news: PM2.5 reached 157 (Unhealthy) at Adarsh Nagar station in early March (AQICN)
Vehicles and industrial activity are Jaipur's primary pollution sources (IQAir)

trends.title: Wind dispersed pollution after spike
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 56 µg/m³ — still 3.7x the WHO limit
hypotheses: NO2 elevated at 87 µg/m³ (vs 65 baseline) — increased traffic or congestion.
news: March winds in Jaipur average 11 kmph — enough to disperse accumulated dust (EaseWeather)

trends.title: Western disturbance brought rain and dust storms
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 28 µg/m³ — still 1.9x the WHO limit
hypotheses: Humidity was 67% (above 50% average) — likely rainfall washed particulates from the air.
Temperature dropped to 21.1°C (vs 24.1°C avg) — suggests a storm or cold front.
NO2 low at 46 µg/m³ (vs 65 baseline) — reduced traffic, possibly holiday or road closure.
news: IMD yellow alert for thunderstorms in 37 Rajasthan districts Mar 18-19 (Patrika)
Western disturbance active Mar 18-21 — winds 30-50 kmph (Zee News)
Jaipur, Ajmer, Bharatpur: light rain with dust storms and gusty winds (Jaipur Unveiled)

trends.title: Dry heat returned after rain relief
trends.subtitle: Peaked at 93 µg/m³ — 6.2x the WHO safe limit
hypotheses: Dry conditions (44% vs 50% average) — no rain washout, more dust resuspension.
news: End-of-March temperatures reach 36-37°C in Jaipur (EaseWeather)
Dry conditions after western disturbance passed — dust resuspension

trends.title: Brief rain brought overnight relief
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 13 µg/m³ — near WHO safe levels
hypotheses: Humidity was 60% (above 50% average) — likely rainfall washed particulates from the air.
news: Intermittent weather activity continued across Rajasthan (IMD)

trends.title: Thunderstorm and hail swept pollution away
trends.subtitle: Dropped to 29 µg/m³ — still 1.9x the WHO limit
hypotheses: SO2 elevated at 9.9 µg/m³ (vs 6.3 baseline) — industrial emissions or power generation.
Ozone elevated at 57 µg/m³ — sunny conditions driving photochemical smog.
news: IMD storm warning: rain, hail, winds up to 85 kmph in Rajasthan on Apr 9 (NewsX)
PM2.5 dropped to 25 µg/m³ (Good) on April 10 after the storm (AQI.in)

narrative: Jaipur's air is shaped by the Thar Desert. PM2.5 swung from 13 to 172 µg/m³ this month — the widest range of any city we track. Western disturbances bring dramatic but short-lived relief; the moment rain stops, dust returns within hours.

stories.event: Desert dust pushed PM2.5 to 172 — 11x the safe limit
stories.impact: The worst single day across all 6 cities. PM2.5 hit 172 µg/m³.
stories.detail: Vehicles and industrial activity compound the natural desert dust. Adarsh Nagar station recorded 'Unhealthy' levels in early March.

stories.event: Western disturbance brought 4 days of clean air
stories.impact: PM2.5 dropped from 48 to 28 µg/m³. One day (Mar 30) later hit 13 — near WHO safe levels.
stories.detail: IMD issued yellow alert for thunderstorms in 37 Rajasthan districts. Winds 30-50 kmph and light rain.

stories.event: Dry heat surged PM2.5 to 93 — 6x the safe limit
stories.impact: PM2.5 nearly doubled from 54 to 93 in one day as end-of-March temperatures hit 36-37°C.
stories.detail: After the western disturbance passed, dry conditions returned. Temperatures above 35°C combined with desert dust resuspension.

stories.event: The cleanest day: PM2.5 hit 13 µg/m³
stories.impact: For one day, Jaipur's air was within the WHO safe limit — rare for a desert city.
stories.detail: Brief rainfall the previous evening likely contributed. But by Apr 1, PM2.5 was back at 75.

stories.event: Thunderstorm and hail: 118 → 29 in 24 hours
stories.impact: The most dramatic single-day drop of the month. PM2.5 fell 75% overnight.
stories.detail: IMD warned of rain, hail, and winds up to 85 kmph. PM2.5 dropped to 25 µg/m³ (Good) by Apr 10.
